papers.paper_scope: Theoretical analysis of the formation conditions for channel segregates in dendritic networks during solidification, focusing on the role of small permeability defects and the direction of interdendritic flow relative to isotherms.

analytical_model.model_name: Percolation model of interdendritic flow with ellipsoidal defect approximation
analytical_model.equation_role: Derivation of criterion [10] for channel segregate formation assisted by small defects
analytical_model.model_note: The model implicitly uses a percolation picture for interdendritic flow and approximates defects as ellipsoidal regions of different permeability. The approximations make the derivation only semiquantitatively accurate, and the percolation model is inaccurate at high fractions liquid. Values for the constant n in equation [10] are difficult to measure.

unmapped_findings: Channel segregates are expected to form most readily at high fractions liquid.

The growth of channel segregates is greatly assisted by the presence of small defects in the dendrite network; since such defects are unavoidable in real castings, condition [10] should provide a more reliable indication of channel segregate occurrence than condition [1].

Channel segregates may form in situations where the interdendritic flow direction is substantially parallel to isotherms, because defects oriented out of the plane of the isotherms can divert interdendritic flow to a direction leading to channel segregation.
This research was supported by the National Science Foundation under grant DMR 79-24268 and by the National Aeronautics and Space Administration under grant NSG 7645.
